_comment: Exercise guide library ('card catalog'). One card per exercise, keyed by the exercise name lowercased + trimmed. Any exercise in workouts.json with a matching card gets a tappable 'how-to' link. Muscle slugs must come from the body map (bodymap.js): chest, abs, obliques, biceps, triceps, forearm, deltoids, trapezius, upper-back (=lats), lower-back, quadriceps, hamstring, gluteal, calves, adductors, neck. video.youtube is a verified-embeddable YouTube video id (check with the YouTube oEmbed endpoint before adding). Cues by Sterling; videos verified by Larry.

db romanian deadlift.name: DB Romanian deadlift
cues: Hold a dumbbell in each hand, feet under your hips, knees soft — not locked, not squatting.
Push your hips straight back like you're closing a car door with your butt; let the dumbbells slide down the front of your legs.
Keep your back flat and chest proud the whole time — never let your lower back round.
Go down until you feel a stretch in the back of your thighs, then squeeze your glutes to stand back up.

bench press.name: Bench press
cues: Lie back on the bench with your eyes under the bar; plant your feet flat and squeeze your shoulder blades together like you're pinching a pencil between them.
Grip a little wider than your shoulders; lower the bar slowly to the middle of your chest, elbows tucked at about 45 degrees — not flared straight out.
Touch your chest lightly, then push the bar back up and slightly toward your face, like pushing a heavy door off you.
Keep your butt on the bench the whole time — no bouncing the bar.

seated cable row.name: Seated cable row
cues: Sit tall with a slight bend in your knees; grab the handle and let your arms reach forward without slumping your shoulders.
Pull the handle to your belly button by driving your elbows straight back, like elbowing someone behind you.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end as if cracking a walnut between them, then let the weight stretch you forward slowly.
Keep your torso still — don't rock back and forth to heave the weight.

goblet squat.name: Goblet squat
cues: Hold one dumbbell vertically against your chest like a goblet, cupping the top end with both hands; feet a bit wider than your shoulders, toes turned out slightly.
Sit straight down between your legs like lowering onto a low chair behind you — keep your chest up and the weight glued to your chest.
Go down until your elbows brush the inside of your knees, keeping your heels planted flat on the floor.
Push the floor away to stand, squeezing your butt at the top; let your knees track out over your toes, never caving inward.

neutral-grip db shoulder press.name: Neutral-grip DB shoulder press
cues: Sit tall holding a dumbbell in each hand with your palms facing each other (knuckles forward), elbows bent so the weights sit by your ears.
Press the dumbbells straight up overhead, keeping your palms facing each other the whole way — like raising the roof.
Stop just short of locking your elbows, then lower slowly back to ear height under control.
Keep your ribs down and don't arch your lower back to cheat the weight up.

plank.name: Plank
cues: Set your forearms on the floor under your shoulders, elbows directly below them; legs straight back, balancing on your toes.
Make your body one straight line from head to heels — no sagging hips, no butt poking up in the air.
Brace your stomach like someone's about to poke you in the gut, and squeeze your butt to lock the line.
Keep your neck long, eyes on the floor just ahead of your hands — don't crane your head up.

lat pulldown.name: Lat pulldown
cues: Sit tall and pin your thighs under the pad so you don't get yanked up out of the seat.
Grab the bar a bit wider than your shoulders, hands like hooks — don't death-grip it.
Lead with your elbows: drive them down toward your back pockets, pulling the bar to the top of your chest.
Lean back just slightly and squeeze your shoulder blades down — never yank the bar behind your neck.

incline db press.name: Incline DB press
cues: Set the bench to a gentle slope — about the angle of a zero-gravity lounge chair, not sitting straight up.
Start with the dumbbells at the top of your chest, palms facing your feet, feet planted on the floor.
Press up and slightly together until your arms are straight, then lower until you feel a light stretch across your upper chest.
Keep your shoulder blades pinned back and down into the bench the whole time — don't let your shoulders shrug up to your ears.

cable face pull.name: Cable face pull
cues: Set the rope at about head height and step back so there's tension before you start.
Grab the rope ends with thumbs pointing back at you, arms straight out in front.
Pull the rope toward your eyes, splitting your hands apart so they finish beside your ears — like doing a double bicep flex.
Lead with your elbows high and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the end; keep it slow, no swinging.

farmer carry.name: Farmer carry
cues: Stand tall between two heavy dumbbells, hinge down, and grab one in each hand with a firm grip.
Stand up by driving through your legs, chest proud, shoulders pulled back — not hunched over the weights.
Walk slow and steady with short controlled steps, like you're balancing a book on your head.
Brace your stomach hard so the weights don't make you tip side to side — don't let them swing.

flat db bench press.name: Flat DB bench press
cues: Lie back on the bench, feet flat on the floor, a dumbbell in each hand resting on your chest.
Press both dumbbells straight up over your chest until your arms are nearly straight — don't slam your elbows locked.
Keep your elbows about halfway out, not flared straight out to the sides — imagine making an arrow shape, not a T.
Lower slowly until the dumbbells are level with your chest, feel a gentle stretch, then press back up.

one-arm db row.name: One-arm DB row
cues: Put one knee and the same-side hand on the bench so your back is flat like a tabletop; the other foot stays on the floor.
Let the dumbbell hang straight down toward the floor at arm's length.
Pull it up toward your hip — not your chest — driving your elbow toward the ceiling like you're starting a lawnmower.
Squeeze your shoulder blade in at the top, then lower it all the way down under control. Don't twist your torso to cheat it up.

db lateral raise.name: DB lateral raise
cues: Stand tall, a dumbbell in each hand at your sides, a slight bend in your elbows like you're holding two water jugs.
Raise both arms out to the sides until they're level with your shoulders — make a T shape, no higher.
Lead with your elbows, not your hands; imagine pouring out a jug at the top so your pinky tips slightly up.
Lower slowly and with control — don't let gravity drop them. Keep it smooth, no swinging or shrugging up by your ears.

db curl.name: DB curl
cues: Stand tall, a dumbbell in each hand hanging at your sides, palms facing forward.
Curl the weights up toward your shoulders by bending only at the elbow — keep your upper arms pinned to your sides.
Keep your elbows still and your wrists straight; don't swing your body or rock back to heave the weight up.
Squeeze at the top, then lower all the way down slowly until your arms are straight again.

rope triceps pushdown.name: Rope triceps pushdown
cues: Stand facing the cable machine with the rope attached up high; grab one end of the rope in each hand.
Tuck your elbows tight against your sides and keep them there — they're the hinge and they don't move.
Push the rope straight down until your arms are fully straight, spreading the rope ends apart at the bottom.
Let it rise back up slowly only until your forearms are about level — don't let your elbows drift forward or flare out.

push-up amrap.name: Push-up AMRAP
cues: Hands on the floor a little wider than your shoulders, body in one straight line from head to heels — like a moving plank.
Squeeze your belly and glutes so your hips don't sag or pike up; your whole body moves as one stiff board.
Lower yourself until your chest is just above the floor, keeping your elbows angled back at roughly 45 degrees, not flared straight out.
Push the floor away to come back up. AMRAP means do as many good reps as you can — stop the set when your form starts breaking, not before and not after.
